preFix.title: Insulating a dormer cheek wall and ceiling
preFix.subtitle: Fitting rigid insulation inside the stud framing of a dormer extension
preFix.excerpt: An uninsulated dormer cheek wall typically has a U-value of 0.9 to 1.2 W/m2K. Fitting PIR boards between the studs, then adding a second layer across the stud faces, brings it down to around 0.18 W/m2K, the Part L1B target for existing walls. The project suits a two-day weekend for one person with a helper.
preFix.sourceNotes: gov.uk Building Regulations Part L1B (2022) 0.18 W/m2K existing-dwelling wall target. CIBSE Guide A for thermal bridging corrections. BRE Report BR443 for U-value calculation conventions.

content.text: A dormer extension adds floor area to a loft room but exposes three surfaces to outside temperatures: the vertical cheek walls, the front face above the window, and the flat ceiling over the box. Without insulation these surfaces typically sit at U-values of 0.9 to 1.2 W/m2K, well above the Part L1B target of 0.18 W/m2K. Fitting
PIR board
in two layers within the dormer stud framing closes most of that gap without taking the structure apart.

attrs.title: Cold roof ceiling vs warm deck

content.text: The flat ceiling of most dormers is a
cold roof

: insulation fills the ceiling joists and a ventilated void sits above. If the dormer roof is being stripped and re-covered at the same time, converting to a warm deck with insulation above the structural deck eliminates the need to manage that void. This tutorial covers the interior cold-roof approach.
What you need
PIR rigid board, 75 mm thick for the between-stud layer plus 25 to 50 mm for the second counter-batten layer. Measure the stud-bay area and add 15 percent for waste and short cuts.
Vapour control layer membrane (1.2 m wide rolls) to cover the warm face of the insulation across the entire cheek and ceiling area.
VCL tape or foil tape rated for at least 100 mm laps, for sealing all membrane joints and perimeter edges.
Softwood counter battens, 25 x 50 mm, to hold the second PIR layer flat against the stud faces before boarding. Fix at 400 to 600 mm centres.
12.5 mm plasterboard for relining after insulation, if the existing board has been removed. Ring-shank drywall screws, 55 mm, for fixing to battens.

Wear an FFP2 dust mask, eye protection, and long sleeves when cutting PIR board. Before opening any stud bay, use a cable detector to locate cables behind the existing board. Do not drill or cut until cables are confirmed clear.
Preparation

Remove the existing plasterboard or lining from the cheek walls and ceiling. Most dormer studs are 75 to 100 mm deep, which accepts a 75 mm first layer flush with the stud face, leaving room for a second counter-batten layer inside. If the existing board is in poor condition, strip it and start fresh.

Mark the positions of any cables or pipes in the stud bays. Work around them by cutting the PIR board in two pieces per bay and fitting them either side, rather than trapping the service behind the insulation.

Check that the dormer cheek has a breather membrane between the stud framing and the outer cladding or tile hanging. If no breather membrane is visible, maintain a 25 mm ventilated void between the back of the outer cladding and the insulation to allow moisture to escape.
Fitting the insulation

Cut PIR boards to a snug fit between the studs. The board face should sit flush with the inside face of the stud, within 5 mm on each side. Larger gaps allow a convection current that bypasses the insulation.

Fix counter battens (25 x 50 mm) horizontally across the stud faces at 600 mm centres. Use 75 mm screws through the batten and into the stud so the fixing bites at least 40 mm into the stud. These battens carry the second PIR layer and the final plasterboard.
Fit the second PIR layer flat against the counter battens. This layer overlaps the stud faces and eliminates the
thermal bridging
from the first layer, improving the whole-assembly U-value by around 15 percent.
Hang the
vapour control layer

across the warm face of the inner PIR layer. Lap all joints by a minimum of 100 mm and tape every lap. Tape the membrane perimeter to the floor and to the ceiling abutment using VCL tape pressed firmly into the corner.
Board over with 12.5 mm plasterboard screwed into the counter battens at 300 mm centres. Tape and skim the joints before decorating.
What done well looks like

The cheek walls and flat ceiling are fully boarded with an unbroken vapour control layer behind the plasterboard. The room holds heat noticeably better than before insulation. A thermal camera on a cold day shows no cold streaks running down the stud lines. The finished surface is plumb and the joints are neatly skimmed.
What done badly looks like

The vapour control layer is left untaped at the floor and ceiling junction, allowing warm moist air to pass around the membrane and condense on the cold outer structure. The studs remain unbroken thermal bridges because no second layer was fitted, capping the improvement 15 percent short of the target. Insulation is cut short in bay corners, leaving 40 to 50 mm air pockets that circulate cold air across the warm face of the insulation.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Confirm there is at least 25 mm of clear air path from the eaves to the ridge vent. If blocking pieces have been fitted during the insulation work, drill 25 mm holes through them to restore the ventilation pathway.
attrs.cause: Insufficient ventilation in the cold void above the ceiling joists
attrs.symptom: Condensation visible on the underside of the flat ceiling deck

attrs.fix: Use 100 mm screws to ensure the fixing passes through the plasterboard (12.5 mm), two PIR layers (up to 125 mm combined), and the batten (50 mm), and bites into the stud. Mark stud positions on the finished board before boarding begins.
attrs.cause: Screws only bite into the PIR board rather than into the studs behind
attrs.symptom: Plasterboard pulling away from the counter battens after a few months

attrs.fix: Measure the U-value of the front wall and check the window specification. Replacing a single-glazed dormer window with a double-glazed unit typically gives a larger per-pound saving than cheek wall insulation, and should be done before or alongside this work.
attrs.cause: The dormer front wall above the window and the window frame itself were not addressed
attrs.symptom: Room still cold despite full cheek insulation
